title: The Black Swan: The Impact of the Highly Improbable
subtitle: The Impact of the Highly Improbable
author: Nassim Nicholas Taleb
themes: Rare, unpredictable events dominate history more than regular, predictable ones
Human cognition is systematically blind to Black Swans due to narrative fallacy and silent evidence
The Gaussian bell curve is dangerously misapplied to social and financial phenomena
Robustness and optionality are superior to prediction in an uncertain world
Epistemic humility — knowing what you do not know — is the only rational stance toward uncertainty
awards: Sunday Times: 12 most influential books since WWII
New York Times Best Seller (36 weeks)
Second book in the Incerto series
excerpt: A Black Swan is an event with three attributes: it is an outlier that lies outside the realm of regular expectations; it carries extreme impact; and human nature makes us concoct explanations after the fact that make it appear predictable. The central idea is not to predict Black Swans, but to build robustness against negative ones and exposure to positive ones.
